paragraphs.text: Sports Rights Owners Coalition reply to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

SROC members welcome the opportunity to contribute to this call for evidence and is supportive of the Commission's broader objective to ensure a safer online environment for consumers, including through the more effective and extended application and enforcement of existing rules.

The Sports Rights Owners Coalition (SROC) is a forum of over 50 international and national sports bodies and competition organizers committed to protecting the vital contributions sports make to both the European society and economy (2% of GDP and 3% of total EU employment). SROC members are key providers of long-term jobs, and some of the EUs biggest tax-payers. Beyond the cultural and emotional connections that Europeans have with sport, it also drives community cohesion, enhances social inclusion and benefits the health of millions of people across the continent.

evidence_quotes.quote: A large number of social media influencers actively promote illegal pirate services across major platforms

paragraphs.text: A large number of social media influencers actively promote illegal pirate services across major platforms, reaching audiences of several million followers. These individuals openly demonstrate how to access illicit content — particularly IPTV services and live sports streams — without acknowledging the unlawful nature of these practices.

While they do not represent the entirety of the influencer ecosystem, their reach and authority over predominantly young audiences make their impact significant. By normalising and trivialising piracy, they contribute to the widespread uptake of illegal IPTV and streaming offers.

Beyond the clear copyright violations and economic damage caused to the audiovisual and creative sectors, these practices also create risks for users. Many IPTV operators operate as organised criminal networks, generating substantial profits outside any regulatory oversight. Their services frequently expose consumers to personal data theft, malware, and unsolicited or harmful content.
Risks for consumers linked to the consumption of pirated content:

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive advertising and fraudulent payment pages

paragraphs.text: Online piracy, particularly of live content, also poses serious consumer protection concerns. Users who access pirated material face higher risks of identity theft, fraud, and online scams, as cybercriminals often exploit these platforms to compromise personal data: Pirate platforms lack basic protections for minors, leaving children vulnerable to harmful or age-inappropriate content.These websites, and the peer-to-peer networks distributing pirated material, commonly host malware or viruses. Such malicious software can damage devices, steal personal data, and even spread across home or business networks. A 2022 study by the Audiovisual Anti-Piracy Alliance (AAPA) found that a large proportion of piracy-related domains expose consumers to malware, deceptive advertising and fraudulent payment pages often within seconds of access.Pirate services might also lead to: i. Personal data or banking information being harvested through deceptive pirated content. ii. Unsafe or fraudulent payment systems that can lead to credit card theft or financial loss.Payment systems advertised on pirate sites are frequently deceptive. Instead of processing legitimate transactions, funds are often funneled into the hands of criminals through complex money laundering schemes.
Another unseen danger for consumers: Ticket touting

evidence_quotes.quote: anonymously advertise tickets for profitable resale and sell them at top prices.

paragraphs.text: The European Commission should also pay particular attention to practices in the area of online ticket marketplaces and consider stricter regulation (as already implemented in some Member States). The business model of these online ticket marketplaces essentially consists of offering and selling tickets that are not legitimate for such a market. These platforms are used by both private and, above all, commercial sellers to anonymously advertise tickets for profitable resale and sell them at top prices.

evidence_quotes.quote: "short sales," without consumers being able to recognize that these are short sales.

paragraphs.text: This is especially true for cultural and sporting events with high demand, for which only a limited number of tickets are available (e.g., pop and rock concerts, as well as major international and national sporting events). Typically, the first offers for such cultural or sporting events appear on the relevant online ticket marketplaces before the official presale begins, as part of so-called "short sales," without consumers being able to recognize that these are short sales.

evidence_quotes.quote: significantly inflated prices on corresponding online ticket marketplaces.

paragraphs.text: After the official sale begins, a large portion of the freely available tickets are purchased by intermediaries and secondary market traders, particularly through the use of automated technical devices (so-called "bots") and through the creation and use of false identities, in order to then anonymously resell the tickets acquired in this way at significantly inflated prices on corresponding online ticket marketplaces. This problem has not been solved by the amendments within the EU Directive amending Directive 93/13/EEC and Directives 98/6/EC, 2005/29/EC and 2011/83/EU on better enforcement and modernisation of EU consumer protection rules (2019/2161/EU) ("EU Modernisation Directive").
We therefore call on the European Commission to:

evidence_quotes.quote: Prohibit the promotion of piracy-enabling tools and services

paragraphs.text: Prohibit the promotion of piracy-enabling tools and services — including illicit IPTV boxes, apps, and streaming platforms; and

evidence_quotes.quote: Explicitly prohibit dark patterns that mislead users toward illegal streaming or IPTV services

paragraphs.text: Explicitly prohibit dark patterns that mislead users toward illegal streaming or IPTV services, in order to protect consumers and ensure a fair digital marketplace.

evidence_quotes.quote: Adopt stricter rules against ticket touting at EU level

paragraphs.text: Adopt stricter rules against ticket touting at EU level and increase the protection of millions of consumers visiting live events throughout Europe.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
